topic: Two friends both apply for the same job
raw_output:
[story 1]

The crisp, embossed invitation for the Senior Analyst position lay on Maya’s desk, a stark white rectangle mocking her. She traced the corner with a fingertip, the cool paper offering no comfort. Across the room, Liam was laughing, recounting a story about his recent interview, his posture radiating easy confidence. Maya felt a tightness coil in her chest, a dull, persistent ache that had nothing to do with the morning chill. She remembered the late nights spent polishing her resume, the shared ambition that had fueled both their applications. Now, seeing him so effortlessly secure, a wave of something heavy washed over her. She picked up her phone, the screen illuminating the faint shadow beneath her eyes. The silence of the apartment suddenly felt enormous, amplifying the sound of her own shallow breathing.

[story 2]

"You really think you can pull it off, Maya?" Liam’s voice was buoyant, laced with an almost careless optimism as he leaned back in his chair. He tapped his pen against his notepad, a small, decisive sound that grated on her nerves. She stared at the shared application form, the identical font, the same boilerplate achievements. A strange tremor ran through her hand as she reread the section detailing her proposed project. It felt hollow, an echo of a path she hadn't fully prepared for. She shifted in her seat, the cheap fabric of the chair suddenly abrasive against her back. When Liam finally looked up, his expression shifted to one of genuine inquiry, and Maya managed a tight, brittle smile that didn't reach her eyes. "I just hope they see the difference," she murmured, the words sounding thin and reedy in the quiet office air.

[story 3]

The fluorescent lights of the coffee shop hummed, a relentless, irritating drone that mirrored the static building up inside Sarah. She kept glancing toward the door, half-expecting Liam to stride in, radiating that effortless competence she seemed to possess. Sarah fiddled with the strap of her messenger bag, her knuckles white where she gripped the worn leather. Every time she reviewed the tailored cover letter, a knot formed just beneath her sternum, tightening with each passing second. She imagined the hiring manager’s face, the subtle nod of approval she desperately craved. A sudden, sharp intake of breath escaped her. She took a long, slow sip of her lukewarm tea, the bitterness momentarily grounding her, before forcing herself to focus on the task at hand, the weight of the unspoken comparison pressing down on her shoulders.
